subtopic_detail: Baseline metabolic workup before olanzapine initiation
stem: A 26-year-old man with a first episode of schizophrenia commenced olanzapine 10 mg nocte two weeks ago after baseline metabolic monitoring (BMI 24.3, waist 92 cm, BP 124/78, fasting glucose 4.8, lipids within range, ECG normal). His father has T2DM diagnosed at 58 and his mother has dyslipidaemia. He is a non-smoker, drinks two standard drinks on weekends, and is on no other medications. He has gained 1.8 kg in two weeks. The team plans to follow the RANZCP/Lambert metabolic-monitoring schedule for second-generation antipsychotics.

data_table.Observations: BP 124/78 mmHg, HR 76, weight 78 kg, BMI 24.3, waist 92 cm.
data_table.Bloods: Not yet taken at this visit.

lead_in: Per the RANZCP-endorsed metabolic monitoring schedule for second-generation antipsychotics, what monitoring should occur at the 12-week post-initiation review?

options.text: Weight and BMI only; bloods are scheduled at 6 months and annually thereafter
options.rationale: RANZCP schedule explicitly includes fasting glucose (or HbA1c) and lipids at 12 weeks, plus anthropometry and BP; highest-risk metabolic changes emerge in first 12 weeks. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP Metabolic Monitoring).

options.text: Weight, BMI, waist circumference, BP, fasting glucose (or HbA1c), and fasting lipids; repeat ECG only if symptoms or risk-factor change
options.rationale: At the 12-week timepoint the schedule mandates full anthropometric panel (weight, BMI, waist), BP, fasting glucose or HbA1c, and fasting lipids; ECG repetition is not routinely required at 12 weeks unless QTc prolongation risk has changed (new QTc-prolonging drug, electrolyte derangement, syncope). This is the highest-yield repeat-monitoring window because olanzapine-related weight gain and dyslipidaemia peak in the first 3-6 months.

options.text: Oral glucose tolerance test, fasting insulin and HOMA-IR in addition to anthropometry
options.rationale: OGTT, fasting insulin and HOMA-IR are not part of routine RANZCP schedule; fasting glucose/HbA1c plus lipids sufficient at 12 weeks. Would be correct only in research protocols (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Repeat ECG, prolactin, and 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure as a 12-week metabolic panel
options.rationale: Not part of metabolic monitoring schedule; prolactin monitored only on prolactin-elevating antipsychotics (risperidone, amisulpride); 24-h ABPM not routine. Would never be correct as a metabolic panel (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Defer all repeat metabolic bloods until the 12-month anniversary review
options.rationale: Olanzapine carries highest metabolic-burden signal among SGAs; first 12 weeks is when emergent dyslipidaemia and impaired fasting glucose appear. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).

explanation.summary: Antipsychotic-induced metabolic syndrome is the leading driver of the 10-20 year mortality gap in schizophrenia and is preventable with structured monitoring. The RANZCP-endorsed schedule for any patient on a second-generation antipsychotic is: baseline weight, BMI, waist circumference, BP, fasting glucose (or HbA1c), and fasting lipid profile; repeat the full panel at 6 weeks, 12 weeks, and annually thereafter. ECG is at baseline and repeated only if clinical change or new QT-prolonging medication; prolactin is checked when symptomatic. Olanzapine and clozapine have the highest metabolic burden, quetiapine and risperidone are intermediate, aripiprazole and lurasidone are lowest. Intervention triggers are sustained weight gain over 7% from baseline, new HbA1c over 48, fasting glucose over 6.1, LDL over 3.5, or established metabolic syndrome - escalate lifestyle measures, add metformin (off-label), or switch antipsychotic. Waist circumference is the earliest mover and the easiest to track at each visit.
key_points: Baseline: weight, BMI, waist, BP, fasting glucose or HbA1c, fasting lipids, ECG
Follow-up: 6 wk, 12 wk, then annually (more often if abnormal)
Clozapine adds baseline echocardiogram, troponin, CRP
Switch consideration triggered by >7% weight gain or new metabolic syndrome criteria
explanation.pearls: RANZCP metabolic monitoring schedule: baseline, 6 weeks, 12 weeks, then annually - weight, BMI, waist, BP, fasting glucose (or HbA1c) and fasting lipids. Olanzapine has the highest weight-gain and dysglycaemia signal of the SGAs (RANZCP; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

stem: A 34-year-old man with treatment-resistant schizophrenia has been on clozapine for 12 weeks, titrated to 350 mg nocte with good symptomatic response (PANSS reduced from 96 to 68 over the period). He had failed adequate trials of risperidone and olanzapine in the preceding two years. He attends the community clozapine clinic for his scheduled 12-week review and is accompanied by his case manager. Baseline values 12 weeks ago were weight 82 kg, BMI 26.4 kg/m2, waist 96 cm, BP 122/78 mmHg, fasting glucose 5.1 mmol/L, HbA1c 35 mmol/mol (5.4%), total cholesterol 4.6 mmol/L, LDL 2.8 mmol/L, HDL 1.1 mmol/L, triglycerides 1.4 mmol/L. Today he weighs 91 kg (BMI 29.3, waist 106 cm), BP 134/86 mmHg, fasting glucose 6.2 mmol/L, HbA1c 41 mmol/mol (5.9%), total cholesterol 5.9 mmol/L, LDL 3.6 mmol/L, HDL 0.9 mmol/L, triglycerides 2.8 mmol/L. He reports no chest pain or dyspnoea. ANC today is 3.4 x 10^9/L. He is keen to continue clozapine because it has 'given him his life back'.

data_table.Baseline (week 0): Weight 82 kg, BMI 26.4, waist 96 cm, FG 5.1, HbA1c 35 (5.4%), TC 4.6, LDL 2.8, HDL 1.1, TG 1.4.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Stop clozapine immediately and switch to aripiprazole monotherapy
options.rationale: Abrupt clozapine cessation in responder without safety crisis risks rapid psychotic relapse and rebound psychosis; metabolic changes are significant but not life-threatening immediately. Would be correct only in life-threatening adverse event (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Continue clozapine and add structured lifestyle intervention, metformin, and arrange cardiometabolic review; repeat metabolic panel in 4-6 weeks
options.rationale: He has gained >7% body weight (11% in 12 weeks), with new impaired fasting glucose and atherogenic dyslipidaemia. Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic and AMH support continuing clozapine in a clear responder while adding lifestyle support, considering metformin (which has the best evidence for antipsychotic-induced weight gain), and tightening cardiometabolic monitoring; repeat panel at 4-6 weeks tracks response.

options.text: Continue clozapine unchanged at 350 mg nocte and defer the next metabolic and weight review until 12 months from baseline, treating the current panel as within expected limits for the first year of clozapine therapy
options.rationale: 11 percent weight gain with new dysglycaemia and dyslipidaemia at 12 weeks meets RANZCP threshold for intensified monitoring and active intervention, not deferral. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Halve the clozapine dose to 175 mg nocte to reduce metabolic load
options.rationale: Halving effective clozapine risks loss of response; not the recommended first step for metabolic adverse effects (pharmacological and lifestyle adjuncts first). Would never be correct as first step (Source: AMH).

options.text: Order an urgent echocardiogram and stop clozapine pending the result
options.rationale: No symptoms or signs of myocarditis (no chest pain, dyspnoea, fever, tachycardia, raised troponin/CRP); urgent echo with cessation is not warranted. Would be correct only with myocarditis features (Source: RANZCP).

explanation.summary: Clozapine has the strongest weight-gain and dyslipidaemia signal of any antipsychotic. In a clear responder, the framework is continue + intervene + intensify monitoring, not stop or halve (RANZCP; AMH).
key_points: >7% weight gain at 12 weeks triggers active intervention
Metformin has the strongest evidence among adjuncts for antipsychotic weight gain
Lifestyle + cardiometabolic review are mandatory, not optional
Cessation reserved for treatment failure, agranulocytosis, myocarditis, or refractory metabolic disease despite intervention
explanation.pearls: A 4-6 week reassessment after starting metformin gives an objective signal; aim to keep him on clozapine if at all possible (RANZCP).

subtopic_detail: Threshold for switching antipsychotic for metabolic reasons
stem: A 41-year-old woman with chronic schizophrenia has been on olanzapine 20 mg nocte for 4 years with partial symptomatic response (residual negative symptoms, no positive symptoms). She lives independently with case-management support and attends a community mental health clinic three-monthly. Over the past 3 years her weight has risen from 76 kg to 102 kg (BMI 36.1 kg/m2), waist 112 cm. Today's review shows BP 142/92 mmHg (confirmed on repeat 5 minutes apart), fasting glucose 7.2 mmol/L on two separate occasions, HbA1c 58 mmol/mol (7.5%), LDL 4.1 mmol/L, HDL 0.8 mmol/L, triglycerides 3.4 mmol/L. She has type 2 diabetes diagnosed 8 months ago (on metformin 1 g BD), and stage 1 hypertension (on perindopril 5 mg). She has had three trials of structured lifestyle intervention with no sustained weight loss across the past two years. She has never been on clozapine and has not had treatment-resistance formally assessed. She prefers oral medication and has a documented history of akathisia on haloperidol but tolerated a brief aripiprazole augmentation trial 3 years ago without akathisia or activation.

data_table.Comorbidities: T2DM on metformin 1 g BD; HTN on perindopril 5 mg.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next antipsychotic management step?

options.text: Continue olanzapine 20 mg nocte and add topiramate 50 mg BD for weight loss without addressing the obesogenic antipsychotic driver
options.rationale: Topiramate has modest evidence for antipsychotic-induced weight gain and is third-line after lifestyle and metformin; with established T2DM, switching the offending agent is preferred. Would be correct as third-line adjunct (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Halve olanzapine to 10 mg nocte, continue current diabetes and BP therapy unchanged, and review weight, fasting glucose, lipids and BP in 3 months
options.rationale: Dose reduction has only weak effect on weight in established olanzapine-induced metabolic syndrome and risks symptomatic relapse. Would never be correct as primary intervention (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Cross-taper olanzapine to chlorpromazine 200 mg twice daily as a first-generation antipsychotic alternative, continuing metformin and antihypertensive therapy
options.rationale: Chlorpromazine is high-burden for weight, sedation, anticholinergic load and adds postural hypotension; not a metabolically favourable switch. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

options.text: Cross-taper olanzapine to aripiprazole (or lurasidone), maintain metformin, continue diabetes and BP management, monitor closely for relapse risk
options.rationale: She has overt metabolic syndrome with T2DM (HbA1c 7.5%) and >7% sustained weight gain on olanzapine. RANZCP and Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic recommend switching to a metabolically lower-burden agent (aripiprazole or lurasidone, both PBS-listed) via cross-taper, with continued antidiabetic and antihypertensive therapy and close monitoring for relapse; she previously tolerated aripiprazole.

options.text: Refer for bariatric surgery and continue olanzapine 20 mg nocte without altering the underlying obesogenic antipsychotic at this metabolic review
options.rationale: Bariatric surgery may have role in selected cases but is not first-line ahead of switching to metabolically favourable antipsychotic; continuing offending drug persists root cause. Would be correct only after failed switch and intensive lifestyle (Source: NHMRC).

explanation.summary: Switching antipsychotic for metabolic reasons is a routine outpatient decision when sustained weight gain, dysglycaemia or dyslipidaemia develops despite structured lifestyle intervention and consideration of metformin augmentation (off-label, 1000-2000 mg/day). The lowest metabolic burden in available Australian agents lies with aripiprazole and lurasidone; medium-burden options include risperidone and ziprasidone. Cross-tapering preserves psychiatric stability: introduce the new agent at therapeutic dose over 2-4 weeks while reducing the original over the same period. Existing diabetes therapy (metformin, SGLT2i), antihypertensive, and statin prescriptions are continued unchanged - the underlying disease persists even after the drug-driver is removed. Bariatric surgery is considered for patients with BMI over 35 with comorbidities or over 40 alone, who have psychiatric stability. Document the metabolic rationale clearly to support continuity of care and audit.
key_points: Switch agents: aripiprazole, lurasidone, ziprasidone, brexpiprazole have lowest metabolic burden
Cross-taper rather than abrupt switch to limit relapse risk
Continue diabetes and BP management through the switch
Reassess metabolic profile 6-12 weeks after switch
explanation.pearls: Trigger for switching antipsychotic on metabolic grounds: sustained weight gain over 7% from baseline, new or worsening dysglycaemia, new dyslipidaemia, or metabolic syndrome despite lifestyle and metformin. Cross-taper to aripiprazole or lurasidone, retain metabolic comorbidity treatment (RANZCP).

subtopic_detail: Lithium interactions with NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, and dehydration
stem: A 56-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder, stable on lithium carbonate 900 mg nocte for 11 years (level 0.7 mmol/L at her quarterly check three months ago), presents to her general practitioner with a 5-day history of right knee pain after a long day of gardening. Her general practitioner is considering analgesia. She also has hypertension treated with perindopril 5 mg daily and indapamide 1.5 mg daily, and type 2 diabetes treated with metformin 1 g BD. She is otherwise well and has had no recent vomiting or diarrhoea. The general practitioner is aware she is on lithium and wants to choose analgesia that minimises the risk of lithium toxicity. She weighs 72 kg, eGFR was 68 mL/min/1.73m2 last month, and she drinks 1.5 L of fluid per day. She lives alone and is travelling to a coastal holiday with predicted 36-degree temperatures next week, with several days outdoors planned.

data_table.Medications: Lithium 900 mg nocte, perindopril 5 mg, indapamide 1.5 mg, metformin 1 g BD.
data_table.Recent labs: Lithium 0.7 mmol/L (3 months ago); eGFR 68; HbA1c 51 mmol/mol.

lead_in: Which analgesic plan minimises the risk of precipitating lithium toxicity?

options.text: Ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily for 7 days without recheck of lithium level during the analgesic course in this stable patient
options.rationale: Non-selective NSAIDs reduce renal lithium clearance via afferent arteriole prostaglandin effect; combined with ACE inhibitor and thiazide-like risks toxicity. Would be correct only without lithium (Source: AMH).
source_refs: Australian Medicines Handbook (AMH): lithium drug interactions

options.text: Celecoxib 200 mg daily for 7 days as a COX-2 selective alternative considered safer for lithium co-prescription with no level recheck
options.rationale: COX-2 selective agents also reduce renal lithium clearance and are not safe substitute when avoiding lithium toxicity. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
source_refs: Australian Medicines Handbook (AMH): lithium drug interactions

options.text: Naproxen 500 mg twice daily with topical diclofenac for adjunctive local analgesia at the affected joint without lithium recheck
options.rationale: Naproxen interacts strongly with lithium; topical diclofenac has some systemic absorption and does not solve interaction. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
source_refs: Australian Medicines Handbook (AMH): lithium drug interactions

options.text: Switch lithium to sodium valproate 1000 mg daily for one week to allow ibuprofen 400 mg TDS, then return to maintenance lithium dose
options.rationale: Abruptly switching long-stable mood stabiliser for short-term analgesia exposes to relapse and valproate toxicity; not proportionate. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Paracetamol 1 g four times daily PRN, with advice to maintain fluids especially in hot weather and avoid NSAIDs; recheck lithium if she becomes unwell
options.rationale: Paracetamol does not interact with lithium and is the safest analgesic choice. The Australian Medicines Handbook and Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic emphasise avoiding NSAIDs, ACE inhibitor changes, and dehydration in patients on lithium, particularly during hot weather; she has a triple-whammy setup (ACEi + thiazide-like diuretic + lithium) and is travelling to hot weather, so hydration advice and a low threshold to recheck levels are essential.
source_refs: Australian Medicines Handbook (AMH): lithium drug interactions

explanation.summary: Lithium has a narrow therapeutic index (target 0.6-0.8 mmol/L for maintenance, up to 1.0 for acute mania) and is renally cleared, so any factor reducing glomerular filtration or sodium delivery raises plasma lithium toward toxic ranges. The high-yield drug interactions are NSAIDs (reduce renal prostaglandins, lower GFR, raise lithium by 25-60%), ACE inhibitors and ARBs (similar mechanism, raise lithium by 25-30%), thiazides (volume depletion, sodium loss, raise lithium by 25-40%), and metronidazole. Dehydration from diarrhoea, vomiting, fever, exercise, or hot weather is the most common precipitant of community lithium toxicity. Every lithium patient should have a written sick-day plan covering: maintain fluids in heat or illness, avoid NSAIDs (paracetamol is the safe alternative), check level if vomiting or diarrhoea over 24 hours, and the symptoms of early toxicity (tremor worsening, drowsiness, ataxia, diarrhoea). Quarterly lithium level, eGFR, calcium and TSH are standard maintenance monitoring.
key_points: NSAIDs (selective and non-selective) reduce renal lithium clearance
ACE inhibitors and ARBs reduce lithium clearance
Thiazides reduce lithium clearance more than loop diuretics
Dehydration (heat, vomiting, diarrhoea) is the commonest precipitant of community lithium toxicity
explanation.pearls: Lithium clearance is renal: anything reducing eGFR raises lithium levels. NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, ARBs, thiazides, and dehydration are the main acute culprits. Paracetamol is the safest analgesic for short-term use in any lithium patient (AMH; NPS MedicineWise).

sources.label: Australian Medicines Handbook (AMH): lithium drug interactions

stem: A 64-year-old man with bipolar I disorder, on lithium carbonate 1000 mg nocte for 14 years, is brought to a regional emergency department by his wife. Over the past 3 days he has become increasingly unsteady on his feet, with a worsening hand tremor and slurred speech. His wife says he has been 'not quite right', confused about the day of the week, and has fallen twice at home without losing consciousness. He started ramipril 5 mg daily 10 days ago for newly diagnosed hypertension and has had reduced oral intake during a recent gastroenteritis episode. His usual lithium level taken 4 months ago was 0.75 mmol/L. On arrival he is afebrile, BP 138/82, HR 88, RR 16, SpO2 98% on room air, GCS 14 (E4 V4 M6). He has a coarse irregular tremor, dysarthria, gait ataxia, hyperreflexia, and bilateral horizontal nystagmus. No rigidity, no clonus, pupils equal and reactive. Bloods: Na 138 mmol/L, K 4.2, urea 9.8, creatinine 132 micromol/L (baseline 88), eGFR 48, lithium 1.6 mmol/L.

data_table.Neurological: Coarse tremor, dysarthria, ataxia, hyperreflexia, bilateral nystagmus, no rigidity, no clonus.
data_table.Bloods: Na 138, K 4.2, urea 9.8, creatinine 132 (baseline 88), eGFR 48, lithium 1.6 mmol/L.

lead_in: Which clinical feature is most specific for lithium neurotoxicity in this presentation?

options.text: Coarse tremor with hand-to-hand variation and intention component on finger-nose testing rather than ataxia
options.rationale: Coarse tremor is key feature but non-specific; combination with cerebellar signs is most specific to lithium neurotoxicity. Would never alone be the most specific (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Cerebellar ataxia with dysarthria, nystagmus and hyperreflexia in the setting of a rising lithium level and falling renal function
options.rationale: The classic neurotoxicity picture is cerebellar (ataxia, dysarthria, nystagmus) with hyperreflexia and progression toward confusion, seizures, and coma as levels rise; it is highly specific when occurring alongside a rising lithium level and acute kidney injury, as here (ramipril + dehydration + chronic lithium). Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic and the Australian Medicines Handbook list this constellation as the diagnostic signature of lithium neurotoxicity.
source_refs: NSW Poisons Information Centre: lithium toxicity

options.text: Fever above 38.5 degrees Celsius with diaphoresis and tachycardia rather than focal cerebellar or dysarthric signs
options.rationale: Fever is not a feature of lithium toxicity and points to NMS, serotonin syndrome, or sepsis; he is afebrile. Would be correct only in NMS/serotonin syndrome (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Lead-pipe rigidity with autonomic instability typical of neuroleptic malignant syndrome rather than lithium toxicity here
options.rationale: Lead-pipe rigidity and autonomic instability characterise NMS not lithium neurotoxicity; he has no rigidity. Would be correct in NMS (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Sustained inducible ankle clonus with ocular clonus, diaphoresis, mydriasis and hyperactive bowel sounds in a serotonergic pattern
options.rationale: Clonus and ocular clonus are hallmark of serotonin syndrome, not lithium neurotoxicity. Would be correct in serotonin syndrome (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Lithium neurotoxicity is a clinical syndrome that develops over hours to days as plasma levels rise from therapeutic toward toxic. Early features (level around 1.5-2.0) include worsening tremor, drowsiness, dysarthria, ataxia, fasciculations and nausea. Severe toxicity (level above 2.5) produces frank cerebellar signs (coarse nystagmus, dysarthria, ataxia), hyperreflexia, clonus (but without the autonomic features of serotonin toxicity), confusion, seizures, and cardiac arrhythmia. Importantly, plasma levels lag CNS toxicity in chronic toxicity, where total body lithium is high but plasma is only modestly elevated. The diagnostic clue in an established stable patient is the development of cerebellar signs on a background of years of stable lithium therapy - usually triggered by dehydration, NSAID use, ACE inhibitor introduction, or renal impairment. Management is immediate cessation of lithium, IV crystalloid to restore renal perfusion, and emergency haemodialysis for severe toxicity (level above 4.0, or above 2.5 with neurological features, renal failure or cardiac instability).
key_points: Cerebellar + tremor + hyperreflexia = lithium neurotoxicity until proven otherwise
Precipitants: NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors/ARBs, diuretics, dehydration, acute kidney injury
Always check lithium level, electrolytes, renal function, ECG
Severe toxicity (level >2.5 mmol/L or refractory neurology) is an indication for haemodialysis
explanation.pearls: Lithium neurotoxicity is identified by cerebellar signs (ataxia, dysarthria, coarse nystagmus) plus hyperreflexia plus rising tremor against a baseline of stable lithium therapy - far more specific than the fever and clonus of NMS or serotonin toxicity, which point elsewhere (AMH; NSW Poisons Information Centre).

sources.label: NSW Poisons Information Centre: lithium toxicity

stem: A 24-year-old woman is reviewed at a community mental health clinic 6 weeks after a hospital admission for a severe manic episode that was her first lifetime presentation. She was discharged on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily and quetiapine 200 mg nocte, with good symptomatic resolution and a return to baseline function. She lives with her partner and works part-time as an early childhood educator. She is sexually active in a stable relationship and uses condoms only as contraception. She is not currently planning pregnancy but says she might consider it in the next 2-3 years. She has no contraindication to hormonal contraception and no history of thromboembolism or migraine with aura. The Therapeutic Goods Administration (TGA) has issued repeated safety advisories about valproate in females of reproductive potential, and the medication carries a boxed warning regarding teratogenicity and neurodevelopmental harm in exposed offspring. The treating team is reviewing whether her current prescription meets the Australian Pregnancy Prevention Programme (PPP) framework for valproate and is asking about the next steps. She has no documented prior trials of alternative mood stabilisers.

data_table.Demographics: 24-year-old female, sexually active, condoms only, no current pregnancy plan but possibly in 2-3 years.
data_table.Medications: Sodium valproate 1000 mg daily, quetiapine 200 mg nocte (commenced 6 weeks ago).

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step under the Australian valproate PPP framework?

options.text: Continue valproate 1000 mg daily with no change to condom-only contraception; document general teratogenicity counselling and treat as resolved
options.rationale: AU valproate PPP requires highly effective contraception (LARC or combined hormonal) for ALL females of reproductive potential before/during valproate; no change is non-compliant. Would never be correct (Source: TGA Valproate PPP).
source_refs: TGA: Valproate and risks in pregnancy - safety advisory

options.text: Continue valproate and document she has been counselled about teratogenicity; defer the contraception decision to her general practitioner at a separate visit
options.rationale: PPP responsibility sits with prescriber; counselling without ensuring effective contraception and signed acknowledgement does not satisfy framework. Would never be correct (Source: TGA).
source_refs: TGA: Valproate and risks in pregnancy - safety advisory

options.text: Discuss switching to a non-valproate mood stabiliser (lithium or atypical antipsychotic); if valproate retained, ensure LARC or combined hormonal contraception and annual specialist review
options.rationale: The TGA and RANZCP guidance require that valproate in females of reproductive potential is used only when alternatives are unsuitable, with mandatory highly effective contraception, annual specialist review with signed acknowledgement of risks (foetal valproate syndrome, neural tube defects, neurodevelopmental impairment), and an explicit plan to switch before conception. Active reconsideration of alternatives is part of every review (TGA; RANZCP).
source_refs: TGA: Valproate and risks in pregnancy - safety advisory

options.text: Stop valproate today without replacement and re-review in 4 weeks, accepting the risk of manic relapse during the unprotected mood-stabiliser-free interval
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation of effective mood stabiliser after recent manic episode risks relapse; safer pathway is overlap switch. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Halve the valproate dose to 500 mg daily on the assumption that lower doses are non-teratogenic and that full contraception is therefore unnecessary
options.rationale: Teratogenicity and neurodevelopmental risk are dose-related but present at all doses; halving does not satisfy PPP. Would never be correct (Source: TGA).
source_refs: TGA: Valproate and risks in pregnancy - safety advisory

explanation.summary: The TGA-mandated valproate Pregnancy Prevention Programme (PPP) applies to all women of childbearing potential prescribed sodium valproate, regardless of indication (bipolar disorder, epilepsy, migraine). Requirements are: highly effective contraception (LARC strongly preferred - implant, IUS, copper IUD; otherwise sterilisation or dual-method), annual review with a specialist who must sign the risk acknowledgement form, documentation that alternative agents have been considered and unsuitable, and a pre-conception switch plan when pregnancy is desired. For bipolar disorder, alternatives include lithium, lamotrigine, and atypical antipsychotics (quetiapine, olanzapine). The 'only effective option' clause requires a documented switching failure trail. When valproate must continue, prescribe at the lowest effective dose, divide into multiple daily doses to lower peak levels, supplement with 5 mg folic acid daily, and ensure obstetric and neonatal teams are aware in advance.
key_points: Foetal valproate syndrome and neural tube defects in around 10% of exposed pregnancies
Neurodevelopmental impairment in around 30-40% of exposed children
Highly effective contraception (LARC or combined hormonal contraception) is mandatory
Annual specialist review with signed acknowledgement of risks
explanation.pearls: The Australian valproate Pregnancy Prevention Programme mandates highly effective contraception (LARC preferred), annual specialist review with signed risk acknowledgement, active consideration of alternatives, and a pre-conception switch plan when pregnancy is desired (TGA; RANZCP).

sources.label: TGA: Valproate and risks in pregnancy - safety advisory

subtopic_detail: Foetal valproate syndrome incidence and counselling
stem: A 29-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has been on sodium valproate 1200 mg daily for 5 years following two severe manic episodes requiring inpatient admission. She is now in stable remission for the past 30 months and consults her psychiatrist about planning pregnancy in the next 12 months. She lives with her husband and works full-time as a project manager. Previous trials of lithium (terminated due to severe cystic acne and 12 kg weight gain at therapeutic levels) and quetiapine monotherapy (failed at 600 mg nocte with breakthrough mania at 4 months) are documented in her records. She uses a copper intrauterine device for contraception and is up to date on cervical screening. She wants to understand the absolute risks of staying on valproate during pregnancy versus switching to an alternative agent, and asks specifically about the rate of foetal valproate syndrome (FVS) and major congenital malformations. Her general practitioner, treating obstetrician, and psychiatrist are jointly involved in the planning. The TGA boxed warning and Australian Pregnancy Prevention Programme materials are on the table. She wants to know what the Australian evidence actually shows about exposure-related harm.

data_table.Background: Bipolar I, stable on valproate 1200 mg/day for 5 years.
data_table.Prior trials: Lithium (severe acne, weight gain) and quetiapine 600 mg monotherapy (failed).
data_table.Contraception: Copper IUD; plans pregnancy in next 12 months.

lead_in: Which counselling statement most accurately reflects the Australian evidence on valproate exposure in pregnancy?

options.text: Valproate carries an MCM rate of ~1%, comparable to background population risk in Australia; continuing valproate through pregnancy is reasonable without switching mood stabiliser
options.rationale: Valproate major malformation rate is ~10 percent (3-4 times background), not 1 percent. Would never be correct (Source: TGA; AMH).
source_refs: TGA: Valproate and risks in pregnancy - safety advisory

options.text: Valproate teratogenic risk applies only in the third trimester; first-trimester exposure is safe and does not require switching to an alternative mood stabiliser
options.rationale: Major structural malformations including NTDs arise from first-trimester exposure during organogenesis; neurodevelopmental harm spans pregnancy. Would never be correct (Source: TGA).
source_refs: TGA: Valproate and risks in pregnancy - safety advisory

options.text: Folic acid 5 mg daily eliminates the risk of foetal valproate syndrome and major malformations; valproate may be continued safely without other changes
options.rationale: High-dose folate is recommended for women on AEDs but does not eliminate valproate teratogenicity; mitigates not eliminates risk. Would never be correct (Source: TGA).

options.text: Valproate carries about 10% MCM risk and 30-40% neurodevelopmental impairment risk; switch to a lower-risk mood stabiliser before conception, with overlap to maintain stability
options.rationale: TGA safety advisories and the Australian Medicines Handbook quote major malformation rates of approximately 10% (versus 2-3% background) and neurodevelopmental impairment in around 30-40% of exposed children. RANZCP recommends preconception switching to a lower-risk mood stabiliser with overlap to protect mood stability; lithium remains an option in many women despite past adverse effects, with specialist input. This is the truthful, action-relevant counselling.
source_refs: TGA: Valproate and risks in pregnancy - safety advisory

options.text: There is no need to switch because foetal valproate syndrome is reversible after birth with early developmental intervention and folate supplementation
options.rationale: Foetal valproate syndrome includes irreversible structural malformations and persistent neurodevelopmental impairment; not reversible. Would never be correct (Source: TGA).
source_refs: TGA: Valproate and risks in pregnancy - safety advisory

explanation.summary: In-utero valproate exposure carries the highest teratogenic and neurodevelopmental risk of any commonly used anticonvulsant or mood stabiliser. Major congenital malformations (neural tube defects, congenital heart disease, cleft palate, hypospadias) occur in about 10% of exposed pregnancies versus 2-3% background. Beyond structural defects, 30-40% of exposed children have neurodevelopmental impairment including reduced IQ, autism spectrum disorder, ADHD, and language delay. Risks are dose-dependent, accelerating above 700 mg/day. Pre-conception switching with overlap is the standard - to lithium, lamotrigine or atypical antipsychotic in bipolar; to lamotrigine, levetiracetam or oxcarbazepine in epilepsy. Document the conversation jointly with obstetrics or neurology. Frame the conversation in absolute numbers (1 in 10 major malformation, 1 in 3 neurodevelopmental impairment) rather than relative risk, which patients consistently underweight. The TGA black-box warning and PPP framework underpin this guidance.
key_points: Major malformations: about 10% (vs 2-3% background)
Neurodevelopmental impairment: 30-40% of exposed children
Neural tube defects, cardiac, craniofacial, limb anomalies all over-represented
Plan switch well before conception with overlap to maintain mood
explanation.pearls: Valproate in pregnancy carries about 10% major congenital malformation rate (versus 2-3% background) and 30-40% neurodevelopmental impairment rate including autism and ADHD; risks are dose-dependent above 700 mg/day. Frame in absolute numbers, not relative risk (TGA; PRIM&R).

sources.label: TGA: Valproate and risks in pregnancy - safety advisory

subtopic_detail: QRS widening and sodium bicarbonate therapy
stem: A 27-year-old woman is brought to a metropolitan emergency department 90 minutes after intentional ingestion of approximately 50 tablets of amitriptyline 25 mg (estimated 1.25 g). She is drowsy but rousable, GCS 12 (E3 V4 M5), with mydriasis, dry mucous membranes, and warm dry skin. Observations: HR 122 sinus tachycardia, BP 96/58 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 97% on room air, temperature 37.2 degrees Celsius. She has had one brief generalised tonic-clonic seizure on arrival, self-terminating. ECG shows sinus tachycardia, QRS 132 ms (baseline 90 ms), R wave in aVR 4 mm, terminal R/S ratio in aVR >0.7, QT 460 ms. Bloods: Na 138, K 3.8, HCO3 18, lactate 4.2, pH 7.28 on venous blood gas, paracetamol level negative. She has no prior cardiac history and is on no other medications. The team is in contact with the NSW Poisons Information Centre.

data_table.ECG: Sinus tachycardia, QRS 132 ms, R in aVR 4 mm, terminal R/S aVR >0.7, QT 460 ms.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management step?

options.text: Oral activated charcoal 50 g now (more than 90 minutes post-ingestion), continuous cardiac monitoring and serial ECGs every 30 minutes
options.rationale: Oral charcoal is contraindicated with declining GCS and unprotected airway; observation alone misses urgent indication for bicarbonate. Would be correct only in alert protected airway within 1-2 h (Source: NSW PIC).

options.text: Intravenous flumazenil 0.2 mg titrated to a Glasgow Coma Scale of 14 or above as an immediate reversal agent for the depressed conscious state
options.rationale: Flumazenil is contraindicated in TCA overdose (precipitates seizures and arrhythmias by removing GABAergic protection). Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Intravenous flecainide 2 mg/kg load over 10 minutes for the wide-complex tachycardia, with continuous cardiac monitoring throughout the infusion
options.rationale: Class IA and IC antiarrhythmics worsen sodium channel blockade in TCA toxicity; bicarbonate is the antidote. Would never be correct (Source: NSW PIC).

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g over 10 minutes as the primary agent for the wide QRS and tachycardia in this presentation
options.rationale: Magnesium is for torsades from QT prolongation; in TCA overdose the dominant lethal mechanism is QRS prolongation from sodium channel block. Would be correct in torsades (Source: NSW PIC).

options.text: Intravenous sodium bicarbonate bolus with reassessment of QRS, airway protection and seizure precautions, in consultation with NSW Poisons
options.rationale: QRS >100 ms (especially >120 ms) in tricyclic overdose predicts seizures and ventricular arrhythmias and is the classic indication for sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg IV bolus (titrated to QRS narrowing and pH ~7.50-7.55). NSW Poisons Information Centre and Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology specify this pathway alongside airway protection and seizure management (benzodiazepines first-line).

explanation.summary: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose is the highest case-fatality psychotropic exposure in Australia. The lethal mechanisms are sodium-channel blockade producing QRS widening and ventricular arrhythmias, alpha-blockade causing refractory hypotension, anticholinergic delirium, and central seizures - any of these can develop within 2-6 hours of ingestion. ECG signs include QRS over 100 ms, R-wave over 3 mm in aVR, and an R:S ratio over 0.7 in aVR. Sodium bicarbonate (1-2 mmol/kg bolus repeated to QRS narrowing, target pH 7.45-7.55) reverses sodium-channel blockade. Single-dose activated charcoal is reasonable within an hour of ingestion in a protected airway. Benzodiazepines manage seizures and agitation; intralipid is a salvage option for refractory cardiac toxicity. Flumazenil is contraindicated. Phone the NSW Poisons Information Centre (13 11 26) early - they coordinate Australia-wide.
key_points: QRS >100 ms predicts seizures; >160 ms predicts ventricular arrhythmias
R wave aVR >3 mm and terminal R/S aVR >0.7 are sensitive markers
Sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg IV bolus, titrate to QRS and pH ~7.50
Benzodiazepines for seizures; avoid flumazenil and class I antiarrhythmics
explanation.pearls: TCA toxicity is driven by sodium-channel blockade; treat any QRS over 100 ms, any ventricular arrhythmia, or hypotension with 1-2 mmol/kg IV sodium bicarbonate bolus, targeting serum pH 7.45-7.55 and serum sodium up to 150 (NSW Poisons Information Centre).

subtopic_detail: Use of the NSW Poisons Information Centre pathway
stem: A 19-year-old man presents to a small rural emergency department 2 hours after ingesting an unknown quantity of dothiepin tablets (also called dosulepin) found in his mother's medicine cabinet. He is drowsy, GCS 13, HR 118, BP 102/64, RR 16, SpO2 98%. ECG shows sinus tachycardia, QRS 108 ms (no prior baseline), R in aVR 3 mm, QT 440 ms. He has not had a seizure. Bloods are pending. The on-call rural physician is the most senior clinician on site and has not managed a tricyclic overdose in several years. The hospital has access to intravenous sodium bicarbonate, intralipid, intubation equipment, and a high-dependency bay. The nearest tertiary centre with an intensive care unit is 220 km away by road, with retrieval by aeromedical service possible. The patient's mother thinks the bottle contained 'maybe 30 to 60 tablets' but is not sure of the original count.

data_table.Setting: Rural ED, on-call physician most senior on site, ICU 220 km away.
data_table.Observations: GCS 13, HR 118, BP 102/64, ECG QRS 108 ms.
data_table.Resources: IV sodium bicarbonate, intralipid, intubation kit, HDU bay available.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step to guide management decisions?

options.text: Telephone the NSW Poisons Information Centre on 13 11 26 for case-specific advice (TCA risk-stratification, sodium bicarbonate thresholds, retrieval criteria) and document the consult
options.rationale: The NSW Poisons Information Centre (13 11 26) is the national poisons information service for Australia and provides 24/7 specialist toxicology advice including TCA-specific risk stratification, indications for sodium bicarbonate, intubation, and retrieval. NSW Poisons Information Centre and Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology recommend early case-specific consultation, particularly for rural ED clinicians managing infrequent toxidromes; the advice is then documented and acted on.

options.text: Email the patient's GP for a verified medication list and prior overdose history, and wait for a reply before any antidote, intubation or further treatment
options.rationale: Waiting for email response delays time-critical decisions; toxicology advice is immediately available by phone. Would never be correct (Source: NSW PIC).

options.text: Search a general medical app for tricyclic overdose dosing and apply the first dose suggestion found without any specialist toxicology consultation today
options.rationale: General reference apps may not reflect AU-specific advice; clinician-to-toxicologist captures case nuances apps cannot. Would never be correct as definitive (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Discharge home with mental health follow-up because QRS is under 120 ms and observed vital signs are currently stable in the emergency department
options.rationale: Discharge of any symptomatic TCA ingestion is not appropriate; QRS can progress, dose is uncertain, minimum 6-h observation with serial ECGs is standard. Would never be correct (Source: NSW PIC).

options.text: Administer intralipid 20% empirically before further assessment or specialist consultation given the wide-complex tachycardia on the current rhythm strip
options.rationale: Intralipid is for refractory cardiovascular collapse from local anaesthetic or some lipophilic drug toxicities; not first-line in stable TCA. Would be correct only in refractory collapse (Source: NSW PIC).

explanation.summary: The NSW Poisons Information Centre (13 11 26) is the Australian 24/7 toxicology phone service that supports rural and metropolitan clinicians; early consultation is the standard of care for any significant overdose (NSW Poisons Information Centre).
key_points: 13 11 26 covers all of Australia, 24/7, free to caller
Use early - the advice helps with disposition and antidote thresholds
Document the consult ID and recommendations
Retrieval decisions for rural patients are part of the consult
explanation.pearls: Save 13 11 26 on every ED resus phone; it costs nothing to call too early (NSW Poisons Information Centre).

stem: A 37-year-old man with treatment-resistant schizophrenia has been on clozapine 400 mg nocte for 8 months, registered with an Australian clozapine monitoring service (e.g. CPMS or ClopineCentral). His weekly bloods through weeks 1-18 were unremarkable; he is now on 4-weekly haematological monitoring per the standard Australian schedule. Today's FBC: Hb 142, WCC 4.8, ANC 1.6 x 10^9/L (last month 2.4), platelets 218. He has no fever, no sore throat, no mouth ulcers, and no other infective symptoms. He has not started new medications and has had no recent vaccinations. He is otherwise well, of European background (not associated with benign ethnic neutropenia), and has no prior neutropenia.
lead_in: Which combination of zone classification, dose action, monitoring frequency and registry action best fits the Australian clozapine haematological monitoring framework?

options.text: Green zone (ANC at or above 2.0 x 10^9/L) - continue clozapine at the current 400 mg nocte dose, maintain 4-weekly FBC frequency, and make no notification to the registered clozapine monitoring service
options.rationale: ANC 1.6 is below AU green-zone threshold (>=2.0) and triggers amber-zone management. Would never be correct (Source: ClopineCentral Australia).

options.text: Amber zone (ANC 1.5-1.99) - continue clozapine at current dose, increase haematological monitoring to twice weekly, notify the registered clozapine monitoring service so the patient's status is updated
options.rationale: ANC 1.6 falls within the standard Australian amber zone (1.5 to <2.0); the appropriate response is to continue clozapine, increase FBC frequency to at least twice weekly until the count recovers to green, and notify the registry. There is no fever, sore throat or other sepsis flag mandating immediate clozapine cessation, and amber-zone management is well-established to avoid premature loss of a uniquely effective drug in TRS.

options.text: Red zone (ANC below 1.5 x 10^9/L) - cease clozapine immediately, admit for inpatient observation, notify the registered clozapine monitoring service, and document permanent rechallenge prohibition
options.rationale: ANC 1.6 is amber, not red; immediate cessation is wrong threshold and would unnecessarily strip a TRS patient of effective drug. Would be correct only at ANC under 1.5 (Source: ClopineCentral).

options.text: Defer the next FBC for 4 weeks given the absence of infective symptoms, continue clozapine at 400 mg nocte, and arrange a routine clinic review at the next scheduled monitoring appointment
options.rationale: Any drop below green requires accelerated monitoring; routine 4-weekly is unsafe at ANC 1.6 (progression to red zone risk). Would never be correct (Source: ClopineCentral).

options.text: Continue clozapine at 400 mg nocte and add lithium 250 mg nocte as a neutrophil-stimulating adjunct, with weekly FBC monitoring and registry notification of the augmentation strategy
options.rationale: Lithium augmentation to raise ANC is not standard of care; correct response is frequent monitoring per registry. Would never be correct as primary response (Source: ClopineCentral).

explanation.summary: Clozapine carries a roughly 0.8% lifetime risk of agranulocytosis, peaking in the first 18 weeks, which is why Australia mandates registry enrolment (CPMS, ClopineCentral) and protocolled FBC monitoring lifelong. The green-amber-red zone system uses absolute neutrophil count thresholds of 2.0 and 1.5 x 10^9/L. Amber zone requires the same daily clozapine dose but FBC twice weekly and registry notification; benign ethnic neutropenia (common in patients of African or Middle Eastern descent) gets lower threshold rules through the registry. Red zone means immediate cessation, daily FBC, haematology involvement, and registry-coordinated decision about rechallenge - which is generally prohibited once a true agranulocytosis (under 0.5) has occurred. Other monitoring beyond FBC includes weekly troponin and CRP for the first 4 weeks (myocarditis), 6-monthly metabolic profile, and annual ECG.
key_points: Weekly haematological monitoring for 18 weeks, then 4-weekly thereafter
Amber zone (1.5-1.99): continue, weekly bloods until green
Red zone (<1.5): stop clozapine, urgent specialist review
Benign ethnic neutropenia and BEN-modified thresholds may apply with haematology input
explanation.pearls: In Australian clozapine monitoring, an amber-zone ANC (1.5-1.99 x 10^9/L) is continued at full dose with twice-weekly FBC until it returns to green; red-zone (under 1.5) mandates immediate cessation, haematology referral and registry notification (AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

subtopic_detail: Smoking cessation and clozapine level rise
stem: A 45-year-old man has been on clozapine 450 mg nocte for 9 years, with steady-state clozapine level 480 mcg/L (therapeutic range 350-600) measured 6 months ago. He smokes 25 cigarettes per day. He is admitted overnight to a smoke-free general medical ward for a 5-day stay following a mechanical fall, and is offered nicotine replacement therapy (patch 21 mg/24 h plus PRN lozenges) but is not yet enrolled in a structured cessation programme. He has no current symptoms of clozapine toxicity (no sedation change, no hypersalivation increase, no tachycardia, no constipation worsening). The ward team contacts the community psychiatry team for guidance on clozapine dosing during the smoke-free inpatient period and any planned post-discharge cessation.

data_table.Background: Clozapine 450 mg nocte for 9 years; smokes 25/day.

lead_in: Which is the single best summary of the relevant pharmacokinetic mechanism and the appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Smoking induces CYP3A4; cessation will halve clozapine levels and require a dose increase of approximately 25 percent over the inpatient stay
options.rationale: Wrong enzyme and wrong direction. Clozapine is metabolised primarily by CYP1A2 (CYP3A4 minor); PAHs in tobacco smoke induce CYP1A2. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

options.text: Nicotine itself is the inducer; full nicotine replacement therapy on admission prevents any clozapine level change, so no dose adjustment is required
options.rationale: Nicotine is NOT the CYP1A2 inducer (PAHs are); NRT replaces nicotine for craving control but does not replace PAH-mediated enzyme induction. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

options.text: PAHs in tobacco smoke induce CYP1A2; cessation raises clozapine levels by 50-70% over 1-2 weeks; monitor for toxicity and consider pre-emptive 25% dose reduction
options.rationale: Mechanism: PAHs (not nicotine) induce CYP1A2 in heavy smokers; on cessation, induction reverses with a half-life of ~1 week, so clozapine clearance falls and levels can rise 50-70 percent. For a 5-day forced abstinence with planned return to smoking, monitor toxicity and check a level. For sustained cessation, a pre-emptive ~25 percent dose reduction with serial levels is recommended (RANZCP and AMH).

options.text: Smoking inhibits CYP1A2; cessation will lower clozapine levels and require a dose increase to maintain therapeutic plasma concentrations during admission
options.rationale: Direction reversed: smoking INDUCES CYP1A2 (does not inhibit); cessation raises levels. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

options.text: There is no clinically significant interaction between smoking and clozapine metabolism; routine clinical monitoring alone is sufficient for this admission
options.rationale: False; this interaction is one of the most clinically important PAH-CYP1A2 interactions in psychiatry, with documented clozapine toxicity from quitting smoking. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

explanation.summary: Clozapine is metabolised principally by CYP1A2, an enzyme strongly induced by polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons in tobacco smoke (not by nicotine itself). Australian smokers on stable clozapine doses typically have lower troughs than non-smokers on the same milligram dose, and abrupt cessation - common during hospital admissions and in smoke-free mental health units - removes the induction over 1-2 weeks and raises plasma levels by half or more. Untreated, this produces sedation, hypersalivation, postural drop, seizures and a rising QTc. Pre-emptive management for any planned smoke-free admission is a 25-30% clozapine dose reduction with a level at day 5-7 and weekly thereafter until stable. Resumption of smoking on discharge runs the same pharmacokinetic shift in reverse - dose increases are needed within 2 weeks or the patient becomes subtherapeutic.
key_points: PAHs in smoke (not nicotine) drive CYP1A2 induction
Cessation = level rise; resumption = level fall
Plan a dose review and clozapine level on day 7-14 of cessation
Same pharmacokinetic shift applies to olanzapine, theophylline, ropinirole
explanation.pearls: Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons in tobacco smoke (not nicotine) induce CYP1A2, the main clozapine metaboliser; smoking cessation raises clozapine levels by 50-70% over 1-2 weeks, so cut the dose by about 25% on admission and recheck the level at 5-7 days (AMH).

stem: A 78-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her daughter with a 4-day history of progressive lethargy, unsteadiness, two falls, and nausea. She was started on sertraline 50 mg daily 12 days ago for moderate depressive disorder after the death of her husband. She also takes amlodipine 5 mg, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg, and atorvastatin 20 mg. She lives alone, drinks 2-3 cups of weak tea daily and about 1 L of water. On examination she is afebrile, BP 138/76 (lying) and 124/72 (standing), HR 78, RR 14, SpO2 97%, GCS 14 (mildly drowsy but oriented), no focal neurological signs, mucous membranes moist, JVP normal, no oedema. Bloods: Na 121 mmol/L (baseline 138 four weeks ago), K 3.8, urea 4.1, creatinine 72 (baseline 70), glucose 5.4, TSH 2.0, serum osmolality 254 mOsm/kg, urine osmolality 412 mOsm/kg, urine sodium 48 mmol/L. ECG shows sinus rhythm, QTc 432 ms.

data_table.Medications: Sertraline 50 mg (12 days), amlodipine 5 mg, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg, atorvastatin 20 mg.
data_table.Bloods: Na 121 (baseline 138), K 3.8, urea 4.1, Cr 72, serum osm 254, urine osm 412, urine Na 48.

lead_in: Which is the single most likely mechanism of this hyponatraemia, and the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Thiazide-induced hyponatraemia alone; continue sertraline 50 mg as response is not yet established, stop hydrochlorothiazide only, restrict free water to 1 L/day, recheck sodium in 24 hours
options.rationale: Thiazide contributes (and should be stopped) but temporal link (12 days after sertraline start) with euvolaemic biochemical SIADH pattern implicates SSRI. Would be correct only without temporal SSRI link (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - SSRI adverse effects

options.text: Primary polydipsia from her depressive ruminations; restrict fluid intake aggressively to less than 500 mL/day without ceasing either contributing medication
options.rationale: Fluid intake is modest; biochemistry (urine osmolality 412 with serum osmolality 254 and urine Na 48) fits SIADH not primary polydipsia (which would have low urine osmolality). Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - SSRI adverse effects

options.text: Addisonian crisis from missed inhaled corticosteroid doses; give IV hydrocortisone 100 mg and broad fluid resuscitation rather than free water restriction
options.rationale: No hypotension, hyperkalaemia, hyperpigmentation or Addisonian features; aggressive saline could cause rapid correction and central pontine myelinolysis. Would be correct only with Addisonian features (Source: APEG).

options.text: Cerebral salt wasting from occult subarachnoid haemorrhage; treat with hypertonic 3% saline and oral salt tablets rather than free water restriction here
options.rationale: CSW requires hypovolaemia and is usually after acute neuro injury; she is euvolaemic with no neuro insult. Would be correct in post-neurosurgical setting (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: SSRI-induced SIADH potentiated by thiazide; stop sertraline AND hydrochlorothiazide, fluid restrict to 1-1.2 L/day, recheck sodium in 6-12 hours, correction not exceeding 8-10 mmol/L/24 hr
options.rationale: The biochemistry (low serum osmolality, inappropriately concentrated urine, urine sodium >30 mmol/L, euvolaemia, normal TSH/cortisol pattern, recent SSRI start, concurrent thiazide) is classical SSRI-induced SIADH potentiated by thiazide. Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic and the Australian Medicines Handbook advise stopping the SSRI and the thiazide, fluid restriction, controlled correction (not >8-10 mmol/L in 24 hours to avoid osmotic demyelination), and switching to an antidepressant with lower hyponatraemia risk such as mirtazapine.
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - SSRI adverse effects

explanation.summary: SSRI-induced syndrome of inappropriate ADH secretion (SIADH) is a common and under-recognised cause of hyponatraemia in older Australians, occurring in around 5% of older patients started on an SSRI. Risk factors are age over 65, female sex, low body weight, concurrent thiazide diuretic, and the first 2-4 weeks of therapy. Presentation is non-specific: lethargy, falls, gait unsteadiness, anorexia, nausea, headache, and at lower sodiums confusion, seizures and coma. Diagnosis requires euvolaemic hypotonic hyponatraemia (serum osmolality under 275 mOsm/kg, urine osmolality over 100 mOsm/kg, urine sodium over 30 mmol/L) with exclusion of hypothyroidism and Addison's. Management is cessation of the SSRI and any potentiating drugs (thiazide, carbamazepine), fluid restriction to 800-1000 mL/day, and slow sodium correction (no more than 8-10 mmol/L in 24 hours to avoid osmotic demyelination). Hypertonic saline (3% NaCl) is reserved for severe symptomatic hyponatraemia. After resolution, an alternative antidepressant with lower SIADH risk (mirtazapine, agomelatine, vortioxetine) can be tried.
key_points: Check sodium at baseline and 2-4 weeks in older patients starting an SSRI
Stop SSRI + offender (thiazide), fluid restrict, controlled correction
Do not correct sodium faster than 8-10 mmol/L in 24 hours
Mirtazapine has the lowest hyponatraemia signal among antidepressants
explanation.pearls: SSRI-induced SIADH presents within 2-4 weeks of starting in elderly women, especially when potentiated by concurrent thiazide and low body weight; check sodium in any older patient with falls, lethargy or confusion within a month of starting an SSRI (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - SSRI adverse effects

subtopic_detail: Citalopram QT cap and discriminating serotonin syndrome from neuroleptic malignant syndrome
stem: A 69-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance after his wife found him agitated, sweaty, and shaking in the lounge room of their home. Over the past 24 hours he has become progressively tremulous, restless, and confused, with deteriorating mental state through the morning. His usual medications include citalopram (recently up-titrated 5 days ago from 20 mg to 40 mg daily for persistent depressive symptoms despite a previously adequate response), risperidone 2 mg nocte (commenced 6 weeks ago for new psychotic features overlaid on depression), perindopril 5 mg daily for hypertension, and tramadol 50 mg up to four times daily started 4 days ago by an after-hours general practitioner for an acute lower back strain. On arrival he is restless and agitated, GCS 13, temperature 38.6 degrees Celsius, HR 124 sinus, BP 158/96, RR 22, SpO2 97% on room air. Examination shows mydriasis, profuse diaphoresis, hyperactive bowel sounds, tremor more pronounced in the lower limbs than the upper limbs, spontaneous sustained inducible clonus in both ankles (right > left), ocular clonus on lateral gaze, and globally brisk reflexes. He is NOT rigid - tone is normal with no lead-pipe rigidity and no cogwheeling. ECG: sinus tachycardia, QTc 502 ms (baseline 6 months ago 432 ms). Bloods: Na 138, K 4.2, creatinine 86, CK 412 U/L, lactate 2.6, paracetamol level negative.

data_table.Medications: Citalopram 40 mg (up from 20 mg, 5 days ago), risperidone 2 mg (stable 6 weeks), perindopril 5 mg, tramadol 50 mg QID (4 days).
data_table.Examination: Diaphoresis, hyperactive bowel sounds, lower-limb-predominant tremor, sustained inducible ankle clonus (R>L), ocular clonus, hyperreflexia, NO rigidity.
data_table.Bloods/ECG: Na 138, K 4.2, Cr 86, CK 412, lactate 2.6, QTc 502 ms (baseline 432).

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate diagnosis and immediate management combination?

options.text: Serotonin syndrome from citalopram (above the over-65 dose cap of 20 mg) plus tramadol; stop both, give benzodiazepines for agitation and clonus, supportive cooling and fluids
options.rationale: His features (clonus, especially inducible and spontaneous lower-limb ankle clonus, ocular clonus, hyperreflexia, mydriasis, hyperactive bowel sounds, diaphoresis, fever, lower-limb predominance, NO rigidity) meet Hunter criteria for serotonin syndrome. The trigger is the 40 mg citalopram dose (which exceeds the TGA over-65 cap of 20 mg due to dose-dependent QT prolongation and is reflected here in QTc 502 ms) combined with newly added tramadol (serotonergic). Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic, the AMH, and the NSW Poisons Information Centre recommend stopping serotonergic agents, benzodiazepine sedation, cooling, IV fluids, ECG/CK monitoring, and cyproheptadine for refractory cases.
source_refs: TGA: Citalopram and QT prolongation - safety advisory

options.text: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome from risperidone (started 6 weeks ago); stop risperidone, give IV dantrolene and bromocriptine, cooling and fluids, continue citalopram and tramadol
options.rationale: NMS has lead-pipe rigidity, hyperthermia, autonomic instability, very high CK (often over 1000), slower onset; no rigidity here. Would be correct in NMS phenotype (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - neuroleptic malignant syndrome

options.text: Sepsis with delirium and incidental clonus; commence broad-spectrum antibiotics and IV fluids only, with no change to current psychotropic or analgesic medication
options.rationale: Lactate mildly raised and no clear source; focal clonus and ocular clonus point strongly to serotonin syndrome. Would be correct with documented infection (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Anticholinergic toxidrome from tramadol metabolites; give physostigmine slow IV push and stop all anticholinergic-burden medications immediately at this presentation
options.rationale: Anticholinergic toxidrome has dry skin/mucosae, urinary retention, absent bowel sounds; he is diaphoretic with hyperactive bowels. Would be correct in anticholinergic pattern (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Acute ischaemic stroke recurrence with autonomic features; thrombolyse within the window without addressing the serotonergic medication interactions seen here
options.rationale: No focal neurological deficits; symmetric clonus, fever and autonomic features are not stroke. Thrombolysis would be wholly inappropriate. Would never be correct (Source: ANSCS).

explanation.summary: Two key Australian safety items combine here: the TGA citalopram dose cap of 20 mg in adults over 65 (driven by dose-dependent QT prolongation), and the serotonin syndrome triad of mental status change, neuromuscular excitation (clonus, hyperreflexia), and autonomic hyperactivity (TGA; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic; AMH).
key_points: Citalopram max in adults over 65: 20 mg daily (TGA QT advisory)
Serotonin syndrome: clonus (especially ocular and inducible/lower-limb), hyperreflexia, diaphoresis, mydriasis, hyperactive bowel sounds; NO rigidity
NMS: lead-pipe rigidity, hyperthermia, very high CK; slower onset; antipsychotic trigger
Tramadol is a frequent co-trigger via serotonergic activity
Management: stop offenders, benzodiazepines, cooling, IV fluids, NSW Poisons Information Centre consult, cyproheptadine if refractory
explanation.pearls: Lower-limb-predominant clonus with normal tone is the textbook serotonin-syndrome physical sign; lead-pipe rigidity points the other way to NMS (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

sources.label: TGA: Citalopram and QT prolongation - safety advisory
Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - neuroleptic malignant syndrome
